one_line_takeaway: Domain fine-tuning plus a Ranker/Critic agent debate lifts smart-contract logic-vulnerability auditing to F1 0.91 with human-readable justifications, roughly tripling GPT-4's cause-alignment rate, though absolute explanation accuracy (38%) and precision headroom remain limited.
key_finding: iAudit's Detector reaches F1 0.9121 / accuracy 0.9111 on the 709-entry test set, substantially beating all zero-shot LLM baselines (GPT-4 and GPT-3.5 both F1 0.6809 with a degenerate recall of 1.0 because they label essentially everything vulnerable, precision only 0.5162) and beating every fine-tuned baseline including the strongest, CodeLlama-13b-class at F1 0.8936. For justifications, iAudit's Reasoner+Ranker+Critic aligns with ground-truth root causes 37.99% of the time versus 24.13% for the second-best (GPT-4). Ablations show the two-stage design and majority voting both matter: single-prompt training gives only F1 0.8278, and adding call-graph context did NOT help (it slightly lowered F1 to 0.9011/0.9083). The two-stage design beat an integrated label+reason model (which reached only F1 0.8433 even after adding label-only data), because in a joint generative objective the label loss is swamped by the longer explanation sequence.
core_contribution: iAudit, a framework for auditing Solidity smart contracts that combines domain-specific fine-tuning with LLM-based agents to produce both a vulnerability decision AND a natural-language justification. The novel design is a two-stage 'perception-then-analysis' pipeline: a Detector model (LoRA-fine-tuned CodeLlama-13b) first makes a binary vulnerable/safe judgement via multi-prompt inference + majority voting, then a Reasoner model (also LoRA CodeLlama-13b) generates multiple candidate cause explanations conditioned on the Detector's voted label. Because fine-tuning alone cannot pick the single best cause among the Reasoner's ten outputs, iAudit adds two collaborating LLM agents built on Mixtral 8x7B-Instruct, a Ranker (selects/merges the best explanation under 10 stated constraints) and a Critic (debates the Ranker's choice, issuing agree/rerank/merge, capped at 5 iterations). The authors also contribute a data-collection method: 1,734 vulnerable functions crawled from 263 real audit reports (via Solodit), plus a GPT-4-based enhancement pipeline (reusing LLM4Vuln/GPTScan) to synthesize 1,810 benign negative samples that audit reports lack.
threat_model: Defensive tool. The adversary is an attacker who exploits vulnerabilities (chiefly logic flaws, which the paper cites as >80% of exploitable smart-contract bugs) in deployed DeFi smart contracts to steal digital assets; the paper motivates this with a cited figure of ~$7.69 billion in cumulative losses as of March 2024. iAudit is the auditor-side asset: it aims to flag vulnerable functions before deployment and explain WHY, so human auditors can triage. It assumes access to Solidity source (function-level, optionally with caller/callee call-graph context). It does not defend against adversarial evasion of the model itself; robustness/poisoning of the auditor is out of scope.
limitations: By the authors' own account, iAudit's strength is logic vulnerabilities; traditional bug classes (reentrancy, overflow/underflow) are present in training data but are not the target use case and are argued to be better handled by program analysis. Being fully AI-based, the paper compares only against other AI methods, not against static/symbolic tools, so no head-to-head against Slither/GPTScan-style analyzers is reported. On precision iAudit (0.9316) is beaten by CodeT5 (0.9431) and others; the authors attribute iAudit's extra false positives to its 16k context (vs 512-token truncation in the smaller models) exposing more complex code. Even a 37.99% cause-consistency rate means the majority of generated justifications do not match the true root cause. Hallucination is only mitigated (via voting + agents), not eliminated, and the LLM stack imposes GPU/hardware requirements. Threats to validity include data-collection bias and label quality. Note: the abstract's phrase '263 real smart contract vulnerabilities' actually refers to the 263 source audit reports; the reported F1 is measured on the 709-sample held-out test set.

datasets_used: Self-collected balanced dataset: 1,734 vulnerable (positive) functions crawled from 263 real smart-contract audit reports via Solodit + 1,810 benign (negative) functions synthesized with a GPT-4-based method reusing LLM4Vuln/GPTScan; total 3,544, split 2,268 train / 567 validation / 709 test
Vulnerable functions median 49.5 LOC, complexity 18.5; safe functions median 35.5 LOC, complexity 13.5 (complexity KL-divergence 0.1 between classes)
16 randomly selected real logic vulnerabilities used for base-model selection
Two anonymous Code4rena bounty projects used for the real-world case study
quantitative_results: iAudit detection: F1 0.9121, Recall 0.8934, Precision 0.9316, Accuracy 0.9111
Explanation alignment (consistency with ground-truth root causes): iAudit 37.99%, GPT-4 24.13%, GPT-3.5 17.47%, CodeLlama-34b 7.35%, CodeLlama-13b 5.27%
Zero-shot baselines: GPT-4 and GPT-3.5 both F1 0.6809 / Recall 1.0 / Precision 0.5162 / Accuracy 0.5162; CodeLlama-13b F1 0.6767; CodeLlama-34b F1 0.6725
Fine-tuned baselines: CodeLlama-13b-class F1 0.8936 (2nd best), GraphCodeBERT 0.8841, UnixCoder 0.8791, CodeT5 0.8481 (highest precision 0.9431), CodeBERT 0.8221
Majority voting ablation: single-prompt F1 0.8278 vs iAudit 0.9121; individual voted prompts ranged F1 0.8988-0.9098
Two-stage vs integration: integrated label+reason model reached F1 0.8433 / Recall 0.8164 / Precision 0.8723 / Accuracy 0.8434 even after adding label-only data, below iAudit's 0.9121
Call-graph context ablation: with-call F1 0.9011, call+no-call F1 0.9083, both below iAudit 0.9121; Ranker-Critic picked 65% of final reasons from with-call prompts vs 35% without
Confidence calibration: 95% of correct predictions had voting score 1.0; wrong predictions concentrated at lower scores (79% at 0.6)
Real-world case study: found 6 critical vulnerabilities across 2 Code4rena projects, expert-confirmed, one not found by any existing tool ('great finding')
baselines_compared: Zero-shot LLMs: GPT-4, GPT-3.5, CodeLlama-13b-Instruct, CodeLlama-34b-Instruct
Fine-tuned code models: CodeBERT, GraphCodeBERT, CodeT5, UnixCoder (full fine-tuning), CodeLlama-13b-class (LoRA classification head)
Internal ablations: single-prompt Detector, integration models (label-first and reason-first), with/without call-graph context
Prior SOTA context: GPT-4 + RAG vulnerability knowledge (~30% precision, cited from LLM4Vuln)
